coredata.dc:title: pH-regulated CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP sensor array and its fingerprinting on 96-well plates: Toward point-of-use/specific identification and quantitation of six tetracyclines in animal farm wastewater, milks and milk-derivative products

coredata.dc:description: Herein, a “lab-on-an-AIE@Ln/ICP” sensor array was constructed by employing aggregation-induced emission carbon quantum dots (AIE-CQDs) as the guest and Eu/GMP ICP as the host. Based on the antenna effect (AE) and reductive photo-induced electron transfer (r-PET) between CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs and tetracyclines (TCs), the as-constructed sensor produced satisfactorily dual-emitting fluorescence. By combining pH regulation with principal component analysis (PCA), the underlying fingerprinting patterns realized the specific identification and quantitation of six TCs in animal farm wastewater, milks and milk-derivative products. Through the aggregation-induced quenching of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs on test strips, the discernible fluorescence alterations were successfully utilized for developing smartphone-based visual assay. To sum up, the prominent novelty of this study lies in that based on the comprehensive principles of AE and r-PET along with combination of pH-adjustment and PCA, the pioneered sensor assay achieves specifically identifying and sensing individual TCs for their rapid and on-site detection in animal-derived matrices.

(9) Ag@HNTs indicates the Ag-halloysite nanotubes;
(10) DPA-Ce-GMP-Eu indicates the integrated ratiometric fluorescent sensing system consisted by dipicolinic acid, Ce3+, Eu3+ and Guanosine 5`-monophosphate disodium salt hydrate;
(11) Fe3O4@PDA@MIL-101(Cr)-NH2 indicates the metal-organic framework MIL-101(Cr)-NH2 functionalised hydrophilic polydopamine-modified Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles;
(12) BSM-DSPE indicates the basil seed mucilage BSM combined with dispersive solid phase extraction;
(13) DSPE assisted CPE indicates the dispersive solid phase extraction assisted cloud point extraction;
(14) Fe3O4@HOFs-MSPE indicates the self-assembled Fe3O4–COOH@hydrogen-bonded organic framework composites for magnetic solid-phase extraction;
(15) Fe3O4/SiO2/MIP indicates the molecularly imprinted polymer using a semi-covalent chemical imprinting approach for magnetic dispersive solid phase extraction;

(17) ELISA indicates the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay;
(18) CL-ELISA indicates the chemiluminescence enzyme linked immunosorbent assay;
(19) FS indicates the fluorescence spectrophotometer;
(20) HPLC-UV indicates the high-performance liquid chromatography combined with UV detector.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a “lab-on-an-AIE@Ln/ICP” sensor array was constructed by employing aggregation-induced emission carbon quantum dots (AIE-CQDs) as the guest and Eu/GMP ICP as the host. Based on the antenna effect (AE) and reductive photo-induced electron transfer (r-PET) between CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs and tetracyclines (TCs), the as-constructed sensor produced satisfactorily dual-emitting fluorescence. By combining pH regulation with principal component analysis (PCA), the underlying fingerprinting patterns realized the specific identification and quantitation of six TCs in animal farm wastewater, milks and milk-derivative products. Through the aggregation-induced quenching of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs on test strips, the discernible fluorescence alterations were successfully utilized for developing smartphone-based visual assay. To sum up, the prominent novelty of this study lies in that based on the comprehensive principles of AE and r-PET along with combination of pH-adjustment and PCA, the pioneered sensor assay achieves specifically identifying and sensing individual TCs for their rapid and on-site detection in animal-derived matrices.

An aggregation-induced emission carbon quantum dots (AIE-CQDs) were employed as the guest material and incorporated into an Eu/GMP ICP host to develop a “lab-on-an-AIE@Ln/ICP” sensor array for the simultaneous identification and sensing six kinds of structurally similar tetracyclines (TCs) in animal farm wastewater and milk samples. Prominent advantages of the newly developed method: (1) The AIE-CQDs were synthesized by a modified one-step hydrothermal method using crystal violet as the precursor. (2) The pH-regulated responses from the host and guest in CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs were used as sensing elements to construct the “lab-on-an-AIE@Ln/ICP” sensor array. (3) Primary sensing elements were selected based on AE and r-PET between CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs and TCs, leveraging the host's sensitized time-resolved fluorescence and the guest's unique AIE. (4) Fluorescence responses to six TCs were recorded under pH regulation and analyzed using principal component analysis (PCA) for specific identification and quantitation of structurally similar tetracyclines. (5) The CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs sensor array for smartphone-based visual detection on 96-well plates show potential for specific, point-of-use detection of six kinds of TCs residues. (6) Combining AE, r-PET, pH adjustment, and PCA, the CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs sensor assay enables specific on-site identification and detection of individual tetracyclines in animal farm wastewater and various milk samples.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found in the online version.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs) are widely used to treat and prevent bacterial infections in both human and animals owing to their excellent quality, affordable price, and a broad-spectrum activity (Yue et al., 2024). However, their widespread production and applications have led to their release into the environment and animal-derived foods, consequently posing risks to human health, including antibiotic resistance, gastrointestinal diseases, and liver damage (Cui et al., 2023; Li et al., 2022; Wang, Li, Jiang, Yu, & Xu, 2022; Wu et al., 2023). Given the distinctive flavor and nutritional value of milk and its derivative products, the EU, FDA, and Chinese government have set the maximum residue limits of TCs at 0.1 mg kg−1 for our daily consumption (Yang et al., 2020; Yue et al., 2024). The residues of TCs in complex media pose analytical challenges owing to matrix interference and issues pertaining to on-site multi-residual assay (Fan et al., 2023). So far, various analytical methods have been developed to detect TCs' residues in milk, including liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS; Hu, Ben, Wong, & Zheng, 2021), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC; Zergiebel, Ueberschaar, & Seeling, 2023), electrochemical analysis (Sereshti, Karami, Nouri, & Farahani, 2021), capillary electrophoresis (Semail et al., 2022), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA; Wang et al., 2021). Despite their high accuracy, these methodologies are rarely used for outdoor detection due to the need for costly instruments, labor-intensive pretreatment, and skilled operators (Fan et al., 2023). Consequently, it is of great importance to develop on-site analytical techniques for quantifying and identifying antibiotic residues in diverse matrices, particularly in resource-limited rural areas.

Fluorescent sensors have been widely utilized across diverse fields due to their reliability, simple preparation, visual observation, high sensitivity, and cost-effectiveness (Huang et al., 2021; Yue et al., 2024). However, the previously reported fluorescence techniques on the detection of antibiotics often lack the specificity required for practical applications (Wu et al., 2023; Yue et al., 2024). Given the structural similarities among TCs, it remains challenging to accurately identify and quantify them using fluoroprobes (Xu et al., 2021). That is to say, conventional fluorescence methodologies can differentiate TCs from other types of antibiotics, but lack the specificity required for distinguishing individual TC derivatives, such as oxytetracycline (OTC), tetracycline (TC), doxycycline (DOX), chlortetracycline (CTC), methacycline (MTC), and minocycline (MOC). This research gap underscores the significance of accurately identifying various TCs with structural similarities, as their varying activities can substantially impact therapeutic efficacy and safety (Arabi et al., 2022). As a result, it is of paramount importance to engineer sensors equipped with versatile recognition units for the selective monitoring of diverse pollutants (Arabi & Chen, 2022). For example, a chemical nose approach endowed with multiple fluorometric sensing elements has been proposed to effectively differentiate among various TCs (Huang et al., 2021). Analytical techniques like principal component analysis (PCA) produce distinct patterns for identifying and distinguishing coexisting antibiotics. When PCA is integrated with an appropriate sensing platform, the accurate discrimination and quantitation of specific TCs can be realized among their analogs (Yan, Hu, Lin, Zhang, & Shi, 2022).

Requirements for multiple signal reporters in sensor arrays hinder their wide applications, particularly for point-of-use scenarios. On-site detection of antibiotics in food samples plays a vital role in identifying contaminated products and preventing them from entering the market. It reduces the need for sample transportation cost, simplifies monitoring processes and enhances more frequent testing with fewer resources (Arabi et al., 2021). Traditional sensing elements are often affected by aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), thereby compromising the accuracy of fingerprinting. Additionally, minimal fluorescence color changes due to low luminous efficiency pose challenges for differentiation using smartphone-based color scanning apps, thus limiting the effectiveness of sensor arrays under field conditions. Hence, it is imperative for the development of sensor arrays by emphasizing the selection of appropriate sensor elements and designing their interactions with antibiotics.

Infinite coordination polymers (ICPs) are emerging nanomaterials composed of metal ions and organic bridging ligands, and they have earned much plentiful eyes due to their structural adaptability, easy functionalization, modification, and excellent capability for guest encapsulation (Huang et al., 2020). These superiorities render them particularly suitable for sensor arrays and facilitate the rational design of specific guest interactions (Huang et al., 2021; Ma et al., 2023; Ostovan et al., 2022; Yan et al., 2022). Lanthanide ions (Eu3+, Tb3+, etc.) are distinguished owing to their narrow emission spectra, large stokes shift, facile fluorescence sensitization, and prolonged decay times. Consequently, they play a pivotal role in developing Ln-ICP nanoprobes by exceptional adaptable host-guest inclusion capability toward entities exhibiting diverse optical characteristics (Zhang, Deng, Xue, Shi, & Zhou, 2016). Recent interest in Eu-based sensing strategies has emerged from the enhanced emission of Eu upon complexation with TC. This phenomenon is sourced from the antenna effect (AE), in which antenna ligands serve as energy collectors that transfer energy to the coordination center to induce sensitized fluorescence (Wang, Zhang, Liu, & Wang, 2019). Following the discovery of solid-state fluorescence of 1-methyl-1,2,3,4,5-pentaphenylsilole (Luo et al., 2001), various aggregation-induced emission (AIE) carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-based materials have been pioneered based on varying synthetic strategies (Ru, Waterhouse, & Lu, 2022; Venugopalan & Vidya, 2023). By employing tetraphenylethylene with AIE properties as a guest, Ln-ICPs realize dual functions, i.e., unique AIE property and luminescence characteristics of lanthanide ions (Huang et al., 2020). This integration displays great promise for sensor arrays by addressing the ACQ problem on solid substrates. Consequently, AIE@Ln-ICPs are conducive to the establishment of sensor arrays for the high-throughput determination of diverse antibiotics.

Building upon the above analysis, we integrated AIE-CQDs into the Eu/GMP ICP matrix and analyzed the AE of Eu/GMP in the presence of antibiotics with coordinating sites, such as β-diketone groups. Simultaneously, it was confirmed in detail regarding the reductive photoinduced electron transfer (r-PET) between CQDs with electron-withdrawing moieties and TCs with electron-donating functionalities by means of structural analysis, energy level tests, and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The pH-regulated responses of both host and guest entities in CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs were identified and adopted as essential sensing elements to construct an AIE@Ln/ICP sensor array. Based on PCA and TCs-induced dual-emitting fluorescence changes of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs, the subclass of six structurally similar TCs were identified and quantified efficiently.

As depicted in Scheme 1 , a kind of lanthanide metal ICP nanomaterial was constructed based on incorporating CQDs as the guest with non-luminescent Eu/GMP as the host. TCs-induced energy transfer to Eu3+ successfully sensitized the distinctive Eu3+ emission, allowing for construction of a dual-channel ratiometric sensing array (CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs). The relative intensity difference was used to investigate variations in fluorescence enhancement or quenching, i.e., red fluorescence emitted by pH-adjusted, antibiotic-sensitized Eu3+ at 616 nm and blue fluorescence emitted by CQDs at 490 nm (Scheme 1A). The real feasibility of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs for detecting TCs was confirmed in animal farm wastewater, milks, and milk beverages, which possessed comparable performance with the China national standard methods (Scheme 1B).

A series of analytical-grade chemicals were used for synthetic and testing purposes, while chromatographic-grade reagents were employed for HPLC analysis. The detailed descriptions on the materials, instruments, synthetic and detection steps were generalized in the supplementary information. These experimental details were composed of the synthesis of AIE-CQDs (Fig. S1), Eu/GMP ICPs and CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs, DFT calculation, detection scheme of TCs by HPLC-DAD, conditions employed for selectivity and competitive assays, discrimination ability of the pH-regulated CQDs@Eu/AMP ICP sensor array of the fluorescence, and fluorescence images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TCs detected by pH-regulated CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: The sensor array was constructed by introducing 100 μL of as-prepared CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP suspension (10 mg/mL) into 1800 μL of acetic acid‑sodium acetate (HAc-NaAc) buffer solutions (20 mM) at pH 4.0, 4.6, and 5.0 (Fig. S2). Subsequently, aliquots (100 μL) of six TCs (TC, OTC, DOX, CTC, MTC, and MOC) were added to buffer solutions or water samples. After a 10-min ultrasonic shaking, the fluorescence intensities (FIs) were measured to evaluate the stability of as-synthesized CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs (Fig. S3). The FIs of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP remained almost unchanged in HEPEs buffers over 7 days, demonstrating that the as-formed CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP can be stored with stability. As for the batch-to-batch stability, minor changes in absorbance at 490 nm and 616 nm indicated the consistent batch quality of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs (Fig. S4 and Table S1). These findings highlight that the synthetic method and stability of both CQDs and CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs satisfy the subsequent experimental requirements. The optical stability of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs was assessed by monitoring FIs at 490 nm over 7 days under continuous 365-nm UV irradiation. As shown in Fig. S5, FIs decreased from 9.5 × 104 to 9.1 × 104, representing a reduction of less than 5 %, evidencing that CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs exhibited good optical stability. Building upon the optimized full-scan emission spectra, changes in FIs at 490 and 616 nm (F - F0, ΔF616 and ΔF490) were recorded at 360-nm excitation in the presence of TCs. The raw data matrix (ΔF616 and ΔF490 of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs at 3 pH levels × 6 TCs × 5 replicates) was acquired and refined using PCA with SPSS 26.0 software. Subsequently, the heat maps, bar plots, and 2D canonical score plots were generated using Origin Pro 2021. To evaluate the specificity and selectivity of the CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs sensor array, various interferents were fortified at the same concentration of TCs, including enrofloxacin (ENR), norfloxacin (NOR), fleroxacin (FLE), levofloxacin (LEV), ciprofloxacin (CIP), moxifloxacin (MOX), sulfadiazine (SD), sulfathiazole (ST), sulfamethoxidiazine (SMD), sulfadimethoxine (SDM), sulfamethazine (SMZ), sulfamethoxazole (SMX), metal ions (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Fe2+, and Fe3+), as well as biomolecules (L-alanine, L-arginine, L-cysteine, L-methionine, L-glutamic acid, l-serine, PA, TPAL, and PHBA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: TCs detection in animal farm wastewater, milk and milk beverage samples
ce:section.ce:para: The animal farm wastewater was collected from a fish, shrimp and crab mixed pond (Suzhou, China). The milk samples were purchased from Suzhou Family-Mart (Suzhou, China), including skimmed, low-fat and whole milk, as well as four kinds of milk beverages encompassing milk tea, soya milk, soured milk, and calcium milk. Aliquots (30 mL) of the water samples were introduced into a 50-mL plastic tube and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.45-μm nylon membrane filter. Aliquots (5.0 mL) of the milk and milk beverage samples were mixed with 15.0 mL of 1 % trichloroacetic acid (TCA) aqueous solution in a 50-mL centrifuge tube. The resulting solution was ultrasonically shaken for 20 min, centrifuged for 30 min at 12,000 rpm and filtered with a 0.22-μm PTFE membrane to remove impurities such as proteins and fats. To investigate the fortified recovery, 1.0, 10.0 and 40.0 μM of six TCs were spiked into the supernatant and then diluted 4 times using 1 % TCA solution. Hereafter, aliquots (200 μL) of the diluted solution were added to the aforementioned fluoroprobe sensor array, including 1000 μL of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs solution and 800 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer at pH 4.0, 4.6, and 5.0. After a 30-min incubation, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at 360-nm excitation. The fortified recoveries for TCs were evaluated at three spiking levels (low, 1.0 μM; middle, 10.0 μM; and high, 40.0 μM).

The probe solution of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs was pipetted into a 96-well plate, to which TCs solutions at varying concentrations were added and incubated for 30 min. The 96-well plate was then transferred into a modified portable dark observation device under a 365-nm UV lamp. With dimensions of 30 cm for length, 23.5 cm for width, and 30 cm for height, the device could block out ambient light and included a square (12 cm × 6 cm) opening at the top for placing a smartphone. The camera of a smartphone was used to capture the color changes in each well (iPhone 13 pro, equipped with a 12 MP ultra-wide-angle camera). The smartphone's Spotxel Reader APP (Version 1.5.5) was used to record changes in the digital image and determine the RGB and grayscale values (Zhu, Han, Liu, & Sun, 2022). The FIs of sample solutions were effectively recorded by this APP and then converted into digital information. Using SPSS 26.0 software, PCA was conducted to handle the raw data matrix, which consisted of three pH values (4.0, 4.6 and 5.0), six TCs, and five replicates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs and CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Figs. 1A-C, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were employed to characterize both the guest CQDs and the CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs sensor. TEM analysis revealed that the CQDs were uniformly dispersed in aqueous solution, with particle sizes of ∼1–3 nm (Fig. 1A). The presence of distinct lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm suggested a graphite-like crystalline structure, indicative of high-quality CQDs. SEM images showed that CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs preserved the network structure of the Eu/GMP ICP sensor host (Fig. 1B and C). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements indicated that the average diameter of the spherical nanoparticles was 36.52 ± 0.30 nm, which closely matched the 35.97 ± 0.64 nm reported for the Eu/GMP ICPs alone. Before and after the incorporation of CQDs into the Eu/GMP ICP sensor host, the consistent nanoparticle sizes suggested that the addition of CQDs did not substantially affect the overall morphology or structural integrity of the ICP sensor host. These structural analyses support the successful integration of CQDs into the Eu/GMP ICP framework without compromising its physical characteristics, which is crucial for maintaining the sensor's performance and reliability.

The measurements of FIs demonstrated that Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles dispersed in buffer solutions exhibited low emission intensity (black curve, vial a), likely attributable to radiative quenching of the Eu3+ excited state by water molecules (Fig. 1D; Zhang et al., 2018). Similarly, the CQDs produced weak fluorescence in aqueous solution under UV light (blue curve, vial b). Notably, the Eu/GMP ICP sensor host resulted in a significant turn-on emission of aggregated CQDs at 490 nm, producing bright blue fluorescence (red curve, vial c), indicating that the host effectively anchored CQDs with AIE. Besides, a slight Eu3+ emission at 616 nm occurred, implying possible interactions between the guest and the host.

By fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy analysis, peaks at 3420, 3200, 1640, 1350, and 1150 cm−1 indicated the presence of OH and NH stretching vibrations, the presence of C=N/C-O groups from polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, along with CH and C-O/C-N functional groups (Fig. 1E-a; Yang et al., 2022). For the Eu/GMP ICP host (Fig. 1E-c, blue curve), the characteristic absorption peaks of GMP shifted due to the formation of the composite material (Fig. 1E-b, green curve). Specifically, the N7-C8 bond stretching vibration (νN7-C8) in the adenosine moiety and the anti-symmetric stretching vibration (νasPO₃) in the phosphate moiety shifted from 1481 to 1469 cm−1 and 1090 to 1073 cm−1, respectively. Conversely, the symmetric stretching vibration (νsPO₃) of the phosphate moiety exhibited a slight shift from 979 to 984 cm−1. These spectral changes suggested interactions between the phosphate and nucleobase units of GMP and the Eu3+ ions within the composite (Huang et al., 2020). When CQDs were incorporated into the Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles (Fig. 1E-d, red curve), the characteristic absorption peaks of CQDs almost disappeared, especially those at 1640 cm−1, 1350 cm−1, and 1150 cm−1. These observations indicated that the CQDs were likely embedded within the network of the Eu/GMP ICPs rather than being localized on the surface. The absence of these peaks could be attributed to the shielding effect of the host network, which greatly limited the vibrational modes of the CQDs. This encapsulation not only protected the CQDs but also integrated them more effectively into the composite structure, potentially enhancing the overall stability and luminescent property of the Eu/GMP ICPs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis disclosed that typical peaks at 284.8, 401.5, and 533.1 eV corresponded to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 1F-a). HRXPS spectra demonstrated that the CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs exhibited carbon-related chemical bonds at 284.8 eV (C=C/C-C), 285.4 eV (CN), and 286.3 eV (CO, Fig. 1G; Mei, Wang, Wang, Li, & Dong, 2022; H. Zhang, Li, Lu, & Gan, 2022). N1s was deconvoluted into components at 401.5 eV (graphitic nitrogen), 400.4 eV (pyrrolic nitrogen), and 399.3 eV (amino nitrogen; Fig. 1H). As for O1s, the peaks at 533.1 eV (C-OH/C-O-C) and 532.0 eV (C=O) were identified (Fig. 1I; Hu et al., 2021). These data indicated that the as-fabricated CQDs possessed abundant hydrophilic surface groups, thus facilitating good dispersion in aqueous solution and the adjustment of the optical property of Eu/GMP ICPs. In Figs. 1F-b and 1F-c (green and red curves), three typical peaks corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s were observed. The XPS spectrum of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP (Fig. 1F-c) represented a superposition of the spectra of Eu/GMP ICP (Fig. 1F-a) and CQDs (Fig. 1F-b). Finally, TGA analysis unraveled that from room temperature to 189 °C, the loss of coordination water and residual organic solvents reached their peak, confirming the high stability of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs (Fig. S7). Based on these characterization data, it is speculated that AIE-CQDs contain pyridine nitrogen, pyrrole nitrogen, and amino nitrogen, along with amino, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and other functional groups on their surface. The inferred structures of AIE-CQDs and CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs are illustrated in Figs. S8 and S9. These intriguing optical properties of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP provide deep insights into their potential for developing novel antibiotic sensor arrays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs for systematic analysis of antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: As detailed in Fig. S10, TCs with β-diketone moiety are known to effectively coordinate with Eu3+, thereby sensitizing the molecular fluorescence through intramolecular energy transfer (Kaczmarek, 2020). Numerous studies on sensing TCs relied on the AE, although the mutual interference between Eu3+ and TCs was often overlooked in previous literature (Huang et al., 2021). This oversight has now been harnessed to develop sensor arrays where such cross-talk serves as functional sensing units.

In the CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP system, the chemical structures of TCs induced fluorescence changes in both the CQDs and Eu3+ fluorescent sensing elements, paving a way for differentiating TCs' analogs with similar structures. With increasing excitation wavelengths from 300 to 370 nm, the emission spectrum of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs revealed the firstly increasing and then decreasing trends for both CQDs and Eu3+ (Fig. S6). Considering that both the 490-nm and 616-nm FIs were the highest, 360 nm was ultimately selected as the excitation wavelength. When excited at 360 nm, all six TCs sensitized the characteristic emission of Eu3+ within the Eu/GMP ICP host at 592 and 616 nm (Fig. 2A), corresponding to the 5D₀ → 7FJ (J = 1 and 2) transitions (Binnemans, 2015). The significantly differential sensitized Eu3+ by various TCs suggested the potential for Eu3+ to distinguish the different species of TCs with similar structures. Additionally, all types of TCs quenched the AIE of CQDs in the ICP host at 490 nm to varying extents, illustrating their feasibilities for sensing components in the sensor array.

As elaborated in Fig. 2B and C, the significant spectral overlapping appeared between the excitation and emission maxima of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs and the UV–Visible absorption profiles of the TCs. This overlapping phenomenon suggested that fluorescence quenching likely occurred based on inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Zu et al., 2017). To further investigate the potential quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetimes (FLs) were measured in the sole CQDs and CQDs+TC systems. The average FLs of CQDs in the presence and absence of TC were 2.35 and 2.39 ns, respectively (Fig. 2D-F and Table S2). No distinct difference in average FLs demonstrated that the fluorescence quenching of CQDs by TCs followed a static-quenching effect (SQE; Long et al., 2020). This static-quenching mechanism was consistent with the formation of ground-state complexes between TCs and CQDs, where the quenching occurred before CQDs reached their excited state. Understanding the interactions between TCs and CQDs is crucial for optimizing the design of sensors and other applications based on these materials.

The addition of TCs significantly quenched the blue fluorescence of CQDs via IFE, but activated the characteristic red fluorescence of Eu3+ through AE (Fig. 2A). This phenomenon might be explained by the fact that coordination between TCs' β-diketone group with Eu3+ facilitated energy transfer from TCs to Eu3+ (Yin & Tong, 2022). Further, the 616-nm emission of the excited states of Eu3+ were measured to investigate the influence of AE on the fluorescence characteristics of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs after TCs addition. As shown in Fig. 2G-I and Table S3, the rising FLs of Eu3+ by TCs evidenced efficient energy transfer from TCs to Eu3+. Hence, the sensitized luminescence of Eu3+ was chosen as a pivotal sensing component in the sensor array. When sensitized by TCs, the enhanced luminescence and extended FLs of Eu3+ provide a number of benefits for sensor development. These include improved detection limit, enhanced signal-to-noise ratio, and greater selectivity, making the sensor array more reliable and versatile for various applications.

The binding performance was further assessed using the double logarithmic Eq. (1).

where F0 represents the 490-nm FI of the newly developed fluoroprobe; F denotes the FIs at varying TC concentrations; Cq is the concentration of TC, K b is the association constants, and n indicates the number of binding sites. As depicted in Fig. S11, the value of n is approximately equal to 1 (n490 nm = 0.9121, n635nm = 0.8256), indicating the formation of a single binding site upon CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs and TC.

The pH sensitivity of the β-diketone within TCs and its coordination to Eu3+ were pronounced (Fig. S10; Huang et al., 2021). TCs with specific chemical structures, incorporating suitable coordination groups and electron-donating functionalities, elicited distinctly dual-emitting responses in CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs. These observations prompted further investigation into the sensor array's ability to differentiate among TCs' analogs that shared similar structures. No additional absorption band was observed when comparing the UV spectra of hybrid systems involving CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP, TC, and CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP + TC (Fig. 3A), hinting no direct chemical interaction between CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP and TC (black and blue curves). However, TCs' addition to the CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP solution caused a distinct redshift in the UV absorption spectra of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP + TC compared to TC alone, likely due to interactions between Eu3+ and TC's β-diketone moiety. The potential quenching mechanism might result from Lewis acid-base interactions between antibiotics and aggregated CQDs, facilitating the transfer of charges from the electron-donating moiety of antibiotic to the electron-accepting region of AIE gen unit (Ma, Feng, Wang, & Wang, 2017).

DFT calculations demonstrated that the quenching of aggregated CQDs' emission likely resulted from the PET process. Under UV excitation, electrons within the aggregated CQDs populated the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO, −3.89 eV) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO, −2.61 eV; Figs. 3B-C). The HOMO energies of TC (−2.88 eV) and DOX (−3.53 eV) were higher than those of CQDs, which were in general agreement with the reductive-PET theory for the facilitating electron transfer from their HOMO to the singly occupied HOMO of CQDs (Zu et al., 2017). Additionally, the LUMO energies of OTC (−2.94 eV), MTC (−3.17 eV), CTC (−2.94 eV), and MOC (−3.07 eV) were lower than those of CQDs, permitting electron transfer from the singly occupied HOMO of CQDs to their LUMO, supporting the oxidative-PET concept (Chi et al., 2020). These findings validate the underlying quenching mechanism and establish the diverse quenching responses of CQDs to TCs as a principal sensing element for the sensor array. Therefore, based on the antenna effect (AE) of TCs to Eu and the photoelectron transfer (PET) to AIE-CQDs, the intricate cross-reactive behaviors of multiple sensing components enhance the recognition and discrimination capabilities of sensor arrays (Liu et al., 2020). As a straightforward strategy, pH adjustment modulated the functional groups on the fluorescence probe and/or target, directly modulating the sensor array's properties without introducing additional elements (Huang et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2022). To investigate the differential binding affinity between AIE-CQDs and ABs, the binding energies of AIE-CQDs with 8 kinds of ABs were calculated with DFT. As shown in Fig. S12-A-F and Table S4, the binding energies of all six complexes were negative, which were conducive to favorable binding between AIE-CQDs and TCs (H.Y. Wang, Jin, & Liu, 2024). By way of contrast, the binding energies of AIE-CQDs with SMD and ENR were positive, and their absolute values were lower than those associated with six TCs, indicating that the binding affinity of AIE-CQDs for SMD and ENR was weaker than for TCs (Fig. S12-G-H). These findings provide a theoretical basis for the specific adsorption and detection of TCs by CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs.

pH modulation not only enabled CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles to function as individual sensing units but also regulated the changes in FIs at the dual emissions of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs, driven by the AE and/or charge transfer. This underpins the foundation for the development of a sensor array for the systematic assessment of TCs with diverse structures. Owing to possessing lower pKa values (Table S5), TCs are stable under acidic conditions but less stable under alkaline conditions (Jafari Ozumchelouei, Hamidian, Zhang, & Yang, 2020). Because the pKa values of TCs spanned the range of 2.8–9.7, their coordination capacities are low at pH < 3.0, and lanthanide-antibiotic complexes may decompose at pH > 7.0 to form lanthanide hydroxides (Li et al., 2020). Therefore, pH 4.0, 4.6, and 5.0 were adopted to modulate the responses of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP to discriminate six TCs (Kaczmarek, 2020).

The pH-modulated excited state energy (AE) and charge transfer processes enhanced the subtle structural discrepancies among TCs' analogs, resulting in a wide array of fluorescence signals. This approach exhibited considerable potential for the methodical examination of antibiotics with similar chemical structures, when employing CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs as the sensor ensemble. Consequently, pH regulation not only endows CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP nanoparticles with autonomous sensing capability, but also governs the alterations in FIs driven by dual emissions. The corresponding observations lay the groundwork for sensor arrays aimed at comprehensively analyzing TCs with diverse structural configurations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discriminatory and systematic quantification capabilities of the sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: The newly constructed pH-regulated sensor arrays' discriminating capacity was assessed by recording the FIs variations (ΔF = F – F0) of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs at 490 and 616 nm in different pH levels and antibiotic concentrations (Fig. S13). Obviously, the FIs of CQDs were prominently quenched with increasing antibiotic concentrations, whereas those of Eu3+ were progressively enhanced. Under varying pH conditions, the FIs of CQDs in the presence of oxytetracycline (OTC) increased with higher acidity, contrasting with a marked decrease in the fluorescence of Eu3+.

CQDs and Eu/GMP ICP were determined to have zeta potentials of 14.3 mV and − 3.05 mV, respectively (Fig. S14). In comparison, that of the composite CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP was −2.16 mV (Fig. S14 A), documenting that electrostatic attraction occurred between CQDs and Eu/GMP ICP (Chen, Fan, Han, Shi, & Zhang, 2020). The zeta potential of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP varied from 5.12 to −2.16 mV as the pH increased from 4.0 to 5.0, due to charge transfer by protonated/deprotonated functional groups (e.g., -NH₂, -COOH) on the CQDs' surface. The above phenomenon formed a series of energy levels and thus affected the subsequent fluorescence responses (Fig. S14B; Gao et al., 2018).

Both heat maps and bar plot response patterns illustrated the differentiation of OTC, TC, DOX, MTC, CTC, and MOC, underscoring the sensor array's effectiveness in distinguishing various TCs (Figs. 4A, B, D, and E). To enhance the clarity of antibiotic fingerprints, PCA was employed to convert the raw data matrix (ΔF616 and ΔF490 of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs at 3 pH levels × 6 TCs × 5 replicates). As depicted in Fig. 4C and F, two principal components (PC1 and PC2) were adopted to construct a two-dimensional (2D) plot for six TCs at the respective concentrations of 30 and 60 μM, segregating the characteristic patterns into six distinctive groups without overlapping.

Subsequently, we further explored the structure-responsive capability of the CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP sensor array toward various TCs concentrations. As such, a highly sensitive sensor array was acquired for sole antibiotic detection. PC1 was confirmed to be sufficient for quantifying antibiotics across varying TCS concentrations, as PC2 did not exceed 40 % (Yan et al., 2018). Clear separation of six TCs without overlapping phenomenon demonstrated the sensor array's satisfactory selectivity. The linear calibration curves of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP are presented in Fig. S15 for six TCs at pH 5.0, 4.6, and 4.0. The limits of detection (LODs) for TCs were calculated to be comparable to or lower than those reported in recent literature, underscoring the sensor array's remarkable sensitivity. The sensor array exhibited distinct fingerprints to be capable of identifying and distinguishing TCs, even when the fortified concentrations were < 0.32 μM. Good calibration curves were obtained by plotting F616/F490 values against TCs concentrations in linear ranges of 0.32–60 μM. The corresponding LODs were computed to be 0.027–0.097 μM based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (S/N = 3), with determination coefficients (R2) ranging from 0.983 to 0.999 (Table S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The anti-interfering effect and stability of the CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: Selective evaluation was conducted for TCs when added by fluoroquinolones (FQs) and sulfonamides (SAs). As depicted in Fig. S16A, no distinct responses were observed for FQs and SAs, while different degrees of fluorescence responses were exhibited for TCs. Subsequently, selective analysis was performed for common metal ions and amino acids commonly coexisted in the aquatic environment and animal-derived food samples. The fortification of TCs led to the substantially rising F616/F490 ratios by ∼20-fold (Fig. S16B), while minimal changes were observed upon the addition of other ions, confirming the excellent selectivity of as-constructed sensing system.

The color coordinates on the CIE (International Committee on Illumination) diagram, without TC (0.2206, 0.3464) and with TC (0.3536, 0.3543), are shown in Fig. S14C. Moreover, the 3D fluorescence spectrum of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs indicated a substantially higher red color intensity at 490 nm compared to 616 nm in the absence of TCs (Fig. S14D). The addition of TC led to a pronounced decrease in 490-nm color intensity and a drastic increase in 616-nm red color intensity (Fig. S7E). These results confirm that the sensor array developed in this study has specific recognition capability for TCs.

Except for the TC family, none of the coexisting compounds significantly affected the FIs of this sensor (Fig. S17 A and B). Only TCs enhanced the 616-nm FIs due to their similar chemical structures, triggering increases in the F/F₀ ratios, and among them, TC exhibited the most pronounced response. As illustrated in Fig. S17 C and D, the presence of these interfering substances had negligible effects on TC detection. These findings document that the sensing system possesses robust anti-interfering effect and thus holds great potential for analyzing real-world samples.

Besides its various spectral responses, the sensor array's programmable multicolor features were beneficial for the practical assay of TCs. Based on the anti-ACQ impact of the guest CQDs and the host of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs, the combination of 96-well plates with discernible color changes was feasible for point-of-use applications with a smartphone image recognition APP (Chen, Wang, Wang, & Wang, 2023). When TCs were fortified at different concentrations and solution pH, the fluorescence color changed progressively under a 365-nm UV lamp, which was visible by our naked eyes. However, because our naked eyes are incapable of properly quantifying color or distinguishing small color shifts, it is not possible for us to determine the exact quantity of TCs. Consequently, the Spotxel Reader APP (Version 1.5.5) was installed into a smartphone to create a straightforward and affordable sensing platform that functioned as a fluorescence color reader for the on-site and real-time quantitation of TCs.

As we expected, the fluorescence color of CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs on a 96-well plate could be visualized at 365-nm excitation (Fig. 5A). Following the fortification of six TCs, the coloring transitions appeared from blue to red for OTC, TC, DOX, MTC, and CTC as well as weakened blue for MOC at different concentrations and pH levels. These coloring variations were photographed and converted to RGB values using a smartphone, thus constituting a new color data matrix (CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs at 3 pH levels × 6 TCs × 5 replicates). By PCA, the 2D canonical score plot with non-overlapping data points confirmed that the sensor array's color fingerprinting patterns successfully differentiated among various TCs at concentrations as low as 5 μM (Fig. 5B). This classification accuracy is analogous to other antibiotic sensor arrays based on spectral signal variations (Huang et al., 2021).

The corresponding blue-to-red transitions were photographed for TCs at varying concentrations under pH 5.0, 4.6, and 4.0 and converted to RGB values using a smartphone. Correspondingly, a new color data matrix was generated for CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs at 3 pH levels × 6 TCs × 5 replicates. In the absence of TCs, CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs exhibited bright blue fluorescence on a 96-well plate (Fig. S18A). However, the addition of TCs triggered the significant fluorescence color changes, i.e., increasing concentrations in concomitant with color transition from blue to red. At lower solution pH, the color changed slowly from blue to red; whereas at higher pH, the color transition completed more rapidly. As depicted in Fig. S18B-D, three linear regressions were constructed for TCs in the concentration range of 5–200 μM at solution pH 5.0, 4.6, and 4.0. Consequently, this pattern recognition strategy was used to build a new quantitative method that was acceptable for systematic study of antibiotics in real-world samples and showed improved classification accuracy when compared to other antibiotic sensor arrays (Xu et al., 2020).

When illuminated at 365 nm, fluorescence images showed that the color of the CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP sensor arrays changed progressively from blue to red when the concentrations of six TCs rose from 0 to 150 μM (Fig. S19). Notably, the color changes under varying pH and concentration conditions differed distinctly among six TCs. Their RGB values showed that six TCs yielded significantly differential R, G and B values at the same concentration (Fig. S20). These findings facilitate the identification and quantification of various TCs by virtue of smartphone-based visualization.

So far, smartphone-assisted real-time and convenient data readout have presented new opportunities for antibiotic analysis and enhanced the sensor's potential for practical applications (Chen et al., 2023; Fan, Wang, et al., 2023). As exhibited in Fig. S21, the percentage of red (R) increased accompanying with a corresponding decrease in blue (B) as TCs' concentrations went upward. When the relationship between the R/B ratios and the concentrations of six TCs at pH 5.0, 4.6, and 4.0 was examined, a strong linearity (R2 = 0.993) was constructed. Plotting the R/B values against TCs' concentrations achieved good calibration curves with linear range of 5–150 μM and R2 values from 0.983 to 0.999 (Table S7). Interestingly, the slope of this equation closely matched that of the linear regression equation between log(F616/F490) and TCs concentrations. Thus, the smartphone-assisted fluorescent visualization approach provides a simpler and more economical scheme for quantifying TCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TCs detected by CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs in real-world samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the practicability and reliability of the present method, we determined six TCs in the animal farm wastewater, skimmed milk, low-fat milk, whole-fat milk, milk tea, soya milk, soured milk, and calcium milk samples. As depicted in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence spectral signals and color variations for six TCs on 96-well plates were transformed into a 2D canonical score plot, and the separate and non-overlapping clusters for each TC species substantiated the strong recognition capacity of the sensor array in real-world samples. Each detection was performed in triplicate in non-spiked and spiked samples, and the final concentration was expressed as the average of three replicates. At three spiked levels (1.0, 10.0 and 40.0 μM), the relative recoveries (RRs) for six TCs ranged from 93.64 % to 106.36 % with relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 0.88 %–4.36 % (Table S8). The newly constructed fluoroprobe gave the intra-day and inter-day RSDs (n = 6) of 1.01 %–2.67 % and 1.06 %–3.12 %, respectively, thereby displaying high precision. As generalized in Table S9, the concentration ranges of the tested samples underscore the sensor array's capability for qualitative and semi-quantitative analysis of environmental antibiotics.

These findings confirm the satisfactory accuracy and precision of the newly developed dual-emitting fluoroprobe. Recovery experiments were carried out using the conventional fortification approach because TCs' residues were not detectable in the pretreatment samples. Furthermore, it is feasible for high-throughput analysis of coexisting antibiotics by the sensor array on “lab-on-an-AIE@Ln/ICP” 96-well plates, which is inexpensive, simple to store and transport, and compatible with smartphones. The aforementioned analyses highlight the significant potential for point-of-use applications in on-site and specific-recognition monitoring of individual TC species under field conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the newly developed fluoroprobe with previous methods
ce:section.ce:para: This proposed approach was compared to other fluorescence methods for TCs assay in terms of analytical performance. As summarized in Table 1 , the CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs-based sensor array provides a significantly wider linear range (0.32–60 μM) in contrast to other fluorescent methods, such as the LLCDs (0–27 μM) (Mehrabi & Ghaedi, 2023), N-CDs (1–40 μM) (Jia et al., 2023) and the Eu3+@AIEgen (0.096–10 μM) (Shen et al., 2022). Besides, the LOD (27 nM) of this method is much lower than those of LLCDs (420 nM), N-CDs (40.5 nM), and NAC@AgNCs (470 nM) (Zhang et al., 2021), while it is comparable to Eu3+@AIEgen (Shen et al., 2022). Although there are lower LODs of the CDs-Eu3+ (9.6 nM) (Wang, Li, Jiang, et al., 2022), SiCDs@mMIPs-cit-Eu (5–16 nM) (Jia, Xu, Chen, Chen, & Xu, 2021), GQDs-Eu3+ (8.2 nM) (Li, Zhu, Xie, Ren, & Zheng, 2018), Ag@HNTs (4.8 nM) (Xu, Zhang, Jia, Bi, & Zhao, 2020), and DPA-Ce-GMP-Eu (6.6 nM) (Wang et al., 2020), the LRs of these methods are too narrow to meet the detection requirement for wide concentration range of TCs. Especially, the previous methods, based on CDs-Eu3+ (Wang et al., 2022), GQDs-Eu3+ (Li et al., 2018), and DPA-Ce-GMP-Eu (Wang et al., 2020), only can be used to determine total concentrations of TCs, leading them infeasible for identifying and quantifying the individual species of TCs.

In terms of time efficiency, the proposed method requires 40 min, which is superior to all ELISA methods, such as the icELSA (>480 min) (Poungmalai, Buakeaw, Puthong, & Khongchareonporn, 2021), the ELISA (>115 min) (Fodor et al., 2023) and the CL-ELISA (>150 min) (Wang et al., 2019), and comparable to the DSPE method (BSM-DSPE for 39 min) (Nakhonchai et al., 2024), and more efficient than N-CDs (>70 min) (Jia et al., 2023), Ag@HNTs (100 min) (Xu, Zhang, et al., 2020), and DPA Ce GMP Eu (142 min) (Wang et al., 2020).

Compared to fluorescence analysis, HPLC has significant superiorities in high sensitivity and low detection limit. Several traditional methods combined with HPLC analysis possess lower LODs (<0.1 μM; Effting, Urbano, do Lago, de Figueiredo, & Tarley, 2024; Li et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2024; Nakhonchai et al., 2024; Phomai, Supharoek, Vichapong, Grudpan, & Ponhong, 2023) and linear ranges feasible for trace-level detection of TCs. With the aid of smartphones and handheld portable fluorescence analyzers, this proposed method and the previous approaches, based on SiCDs@mMIPs-cit-Eu (Jia et al., 2021), Eu3+@AIEgen (Shen et al., 2022), BNQD-Eu3+ (Yang et al., 2020), Ag@HNTs (Xu, Zhang, et al., 2020), and DPA-Ce-GMP-Eu (Wang et al., 2020), realize the rapid and on-site detection of organic pollutants. However, these traditional instrument-based methods, such as HPLC and ELISA, require large analytical equipment and are typically restricted to laboratory settings. Furthermore, they need complex pretreatment procedures, substantial volumes of organic solvents, high cost, and skilled operators, leading them impractical for the rapid and on-site analysis of antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the data achieved by as-constructed fluoroprobe with HPLC-DAD detection
ce:section.ce:para: A comparison was conducted between the data obtained from the CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs sensor array and those from HPLC-DAD to evaluate the detection accuracy of TCs (Table S10). Fig. S22 shows the typical chromatograms of TCs in the spiked (10.0 μM) and unspiked (blank) samples, including animal farm wastewater and three types of milks. The concentrations of TCs were determined to be in the range of 9.7–10.8 μM using the CQDs@Eu/GMP ICPs-based sensor array, while 9.9–10.2 μM using HPLC-DAD. Consequently, the relative accuracy ranged from 96.97 % to 105.88 %, indicating the superior reliability of this fluoroprobe sensor array. These findings affirm the superior performance of the newly developed sensor array in accurately detecting TCs from animal-related wastewater and foods.

Based on the AE, we successfully constructed a CQDs@Eu/GMP ICP sensor array by combining pH adjustment with the differential fluorescence responses to varying species of TCs. Besides, the fluorescence signal was converted into a 2D scoring map for visually distinguishing six TCs, pertaining to the same family of tetracyclines, by using the PCA method. As such, the similarly structural antibiotics were accurately discriminated and quantified in real-world animal-derived wastewater, milk and milk beverage samples. Furthermore, the identification of six TCs was successfully achieved by the sensor array through the conversion of fluorescent signals into RGB colors with the assistance of a smartphone. Overall, these present approaches open a new avenue for specific identification and quantitation of individual antibiotic species in complex food matrices.
